## [00:00 - 03:03]

All right, it's time to mine that past of yours to bring forward the stories that we've left behind. Bring them forward so that we can remember them, we can contextualize and we can use them in our life. This is a strategy called first, last, best, worst. It is based upon the premise that great stories can be found the first time we do something, the last time we do it, the best time we do it and the worst time we do it. So I need you to go to the workbook or just recreate a sheet that looks like this on a piece of paper. It's a simple chart. You can see that there's a subject line that is the first column and then across we have the first, the last, the best and the worst columns. We're going to fill these in and in doing so, we're going to find some stories from our past. We're going to recover the stories we should have been recording all along. So let's begin. In that first upper left column, I want you to write the word kiss K-I-S-S. And now what we're going to do is we're going to move across the row. And so in the first column, we're going to write our first kiss. Now we can write more than one thing in a box. And if we can't remember something, we can leave a box empty, but probably with first kiss, you probably have one name to put in there unless your first kiss was pretty extraordinary. Mine was a kiss with a girl named Laura in Pasadena, California. That was my first kiss. So I'm going to write the name Laura, you write whatever you want. If you can't remember the person's name, that is a tragedy, but maybe you write the guy leaning up against the 7-Eleven drinking a slushie and he gave me a kiss, whatever it is you write it in. And then last kiss, the last person who kissed you for me, that's Charlie, my son. He gave me a kiss before I came to the studio. And then best kiss, think about what was the best kiss of your life for me, probably two of them. One was Laura in Pasadena. My very first kiss was a pretty great kiss. And then my wife, Alicia, our first kiss in a parking lot was pretty great too. I would list both of those names. And then your worst kiss. If you can think of someone who maybe was a bad kisser or the kiss was at the wrong moment. For me, it was a girl named Sheila who was just a terrible kisser. We dated briefly and quite frankly, I broke up with her because she was a terrible kisser. Go ahead and fill in your row right now. Underneath kiss, let's add another one. Let's add Pat. That'll be our next prompt, so your first pet. You know my first pet was Measelman, the dog named after the doctor who gave my father his vasectomy. ## [06:10 - 09:17]

We could use frenemy. We could use injury. We could use vacation. We could use the word trouble. These are all prompts that generate quick responses and oftentimes a multitude of responses. But what I like to do sometimes is choose something a little more challenging. We tend to find new stories that way. I'm staring right now at a clock in the room. It was a clock given to me actually by one of my clients. Let's use the word clock because I've never used it before. We might not be able to fill all of them in, but let's see how we do. So write the word clock and think about the first clock you can remember. For me, I guess I had a clock radio when I was a teenager and I remember it because the lamp fell on the clock radio and melted the clock radio. That's the first clock that I can remember sort of being important in my life. That's the first one. The last clock is actually behind me. Not sure if you can see it, but it's a clock that doesn't actually have numbers on it. I just got it for the studio and I kind of love it. So that is my last clock because it's the most recently added clock. My best clock, my best clock. I'd probably leave this blank right now because I can't really think of a best clock. I can think of a worst clock for sure, but not a best one. So I'm going to leave that blank for now. For the worst clock, I've got two. When I was in in-house suspension in high school, which I was in a lot, they would put a cardboard over the clock in the room so we never knew what time it was to torture us, to have us keeping track of the bells, to keep track of the time. And then I had a cuckoo clock in my living room for a long time. It was made by my grandfather and I loved it. And then I found out that my grandfather was not very kind to my father when my father was in Vietnam. My father was writing letters to his brothers and sisters and one day he received a letter from my grandfather saying, stop writing to us. Do your job and leave us alone. We'll see you when you get back. And it broke my father's heart and that story broke my heart. And the moment I heard that story, I took the cuckoo clock down. I put it in the basement. ## [09:17 - 12:17]

be a story. And then I want you to put a star next to everyone that returned to you today. In other words, oh, I hadn't thought about that for a very long time. And now it's back in my mind like the clock radio for me. I had not thought about the melted clock radio probably for 30 years. But I gave the prompt of a clock and that clock radio from my teenage years returned to me. I'd put a star next to it. It's a memory I've recovered. Take a moment, pause the video, circle up everything that you think could be a story, put a star next to everything that is newly recovered. And when you're done, hit play. Okay, how would you do? I assume you have at least one item circled. You probably have three items circled, maybe five, maybe more than five. If you had been doing homework for life for a very long time, you'd have many more items circled. Because one of the things that happens when you do homework for life is you see stories where you can't see them right now. When I do this activity with people, I often have more circles than anybody, not because my life is more interesting, but because I am able to see more stories than most people can. If you when I sat down at a table and I started asking you questions about everything in your chart right now, I bet I could pull out stories that you can't see yet. But hopefully you found some stories. My favorite part of the chart though are the stars. I think there's so much value in reaching into the past and finding something that you had let go. There's not much value in a melted clock radio, admittedly. It's not a story. It's not something I even will share with my children at dinner tonight. But when I brought the clock radio into my present, I also brought my childhood bedroom back with me, which is kind of amazing. I can see things in my childhood bedroom that I had completely forgotten about, including the desk that the clock radio sat on. None of these things are stories. None of these things are ideas that I can share on a stage. I can't entertain an audience with them. I can't even entertain my 10-year-old son with them. But they're back here in my heart and my mind. We are the first and most important audiences for our stories. We are the ones that are going to find these things most compelling, most interesting. We're going to be the ones who are happiest about hearing them and seeing them in our minds eye. We do first, last, best, worst to find stories from the past and bring them forward. And maybe some of those things we'll share with other people. And that's wonderful.

## [12:18 - 15:02]

But if you don't share any of them with anyone, you've just taken a peek into your past. You've found some things that you left behind, things you shouldn't have left behind, and you brought them forward into your life today. And I'm telling you that doing this often, doing this enough will make you a happier person. It'll make your life feel fuller and more complete. And you'll have a mountain of stories to tell. Now, if you found a new story today, if you found a new item, and I'm sure you did, I want you to add it to your homework for life, just like I'm going to add the clock radio to my homework for life today. Again, it's going to be one of that 50% of things that never make it to a stage or even ever come out of my mouth. But it's going to go in my homework for life, because today, I remembered a clock radio from my childhood. And I remember the desk it used to sit on. And I remember the television that used to sit in that bedroom. And I remember the ugly carpet and the lazy boy chair in the corner and the closet that wasn't a closet. And the light that had flies inside the lens, but we couldn't pull the lens out so the flies had to stay there. All of those things I remembered about my childhood bedroom by finding a clock radio, nothing I'm going to tell anyone, but all of it I was happy to find today. So take the things you found, add them to your homework for life, and keep playing first, last, best, worst. Build on it when you have five minutes, add a new prompt to your chart, and then go across looking for new ideas, new stories from your past. This is not something you're going to do once. This is not something you're going to do this time and never again. This should be something you're doing often. There are a multitude of stories in your past just waiting to be found. The more of them you find, the more stories you will have to tell, and the happier you will be about the life you have led. So go looking for them often, add to your list, build on the prompts I've given you today. Keep doing first, last, best, worst, whenever you can. Find those stories, make a giant pile of them, and start sharing the ones that are worth sharing with the people you love.
